home.title: Vibe Code Tours — A guided journey into AI-paired coding
home.description: A cohort-based online tour of the AI coding ecosystem, designed for Myanmar developers ready to build with Claude Code. 8 weeks. Free.
home.heroSub: A cohort-based online tour of the AI coding ecosystem, designed for Myanmar developers ready to build with Claude Code.
home.card1Body: AI-paired coding fundamentals, prompt craft, team workflows on real projects, and how to ship something useful with Claude as your pair.
home.card2Body: 2-hour live sessions, twice a week, for 8 weeks. Solo instructor, small cohort, team projects. Public Tour Repo. Demo day at the end.
home.card3Body: Beginners with terminal comfort. Intermediate devs adding AI to their toolkit. Experienced engineers curious about the AI coding landscape, not just one tool.
home.credibility: Free to participants · Sponsored by [list] · MIT / CC-BY-SA licensed curriculum

about.title: About — Vibe Code Tours
about.description: Why Vibe Code Tours exists: a curated entry into the AI coding ecosystem for Myanmar developers, built on awareness over mastery and honesty about what we skip.
about.heading: Why this Tour exists
about.intro1: Most coding courses teach one stack. Most AI tutorials teach one tool. Neither helps a developer in 2026, when the landscape changes monthly and the meta-skill is "navigate change."
about.intro2: We started this Tour for a specific group: Myanmar developers who can follow a terminal, want to build, and need a curated entry into the AI coding ecosystem without paying $1000 for a bootcamp that teaches yesterday's tools.

beliefs.title: Awareness over mastery.
beliefs.body: You don't need to master Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, and four others. You need to know they exist, what they're for, and how to pick. Mastery comes after.

beliefs.title: AI is the default partner, not an afterthought.
beliefs.body: Claude shows up in every chapter — including the team workflows chapter, the deployment chapter, the testing chapter. Not just the "AI module."

beliefs.title: Projects are vehicles, not outcomes.
beliefs.body: 60% finished with insight beats 100% finished without. The point is what you understand, not what you shipped.

beliefs.title: Honest about what we skip.
beliefs.body: We don't teach a specific framework deeply. We don't certify. We don't place students in jobs. We tell you what we're NOT doing as clearly as what we ARE.

beliefs.title: Teaching is recursive.
beliefs.body: Each cohort makes the next cohort better. We publish what we learn. We publish what didn't work.

notList: A bootcamp with a placement guarantee — there is no placement
An AI coding tools advertisement — we critique tools openly
A pure Claude Code class — Claude is the through-line, not the topic
A beginner-only space — we welcome all levels, with peer-teaching as design
A Myanmar-exclusive program — applicants from any country welcome, but instruction is in English with Burmese support

team.title: Team — Vibe Code Tours
team.description: Who runs Vibe Code Tours: the lead instructor, advisors, and how to help.
team.heading: Who runs this
team.name: kokoye2007
team.bio: Lead instructor of Vibe Code Tours. I build with AI coding tools and run this cohort to help Myanmar developers find their footing in the fast-moving AI coding landscape — awareness over mastery, AI as the default pair. Most of my work lives on GitHub.
team.helpBody: We accept contributions of feedback, sponsorship, and time. See the sponsors page for how to contribute.

curriculum.title: Curriculum — Vibe Code Tours
curriculum.description: 8 chapters over 8 weeks. AI-paired coding from setup to demo day, structured by the A/B/C tier framework.
curriculum.heading: What you'll learn
curriculum.startDate: Cohort 2 begins October 2026. Applications open now.
curriculum.intro: 8 chapters over 8 weeks. 2 sessions × 2 hours per chapter. Plus async pre-class setup (Chapter 0). Cohort runs 16 live sessions across the 8-week arc.
curriculum.tierIntro: Every chapter is built from blocks tagged by tier. The mix shows how AI fits into that chapter:
curriculum.tierA: Tier A — AI off. Manual demo, bootstrap, human ceremony.
curriculum.tierB: Tier B — Foundation. You do it manually first, then with AI.
curriculum.tierC: Tier C — AI default. Claude does the work, you validate.
curriculum.tierNote: We deliberately balance the ratio per chapter. Ch0 is all A (manual setup). Ch2 is heavy B+C (learning Claude). Ch8 is back to A (demos are human).
get: A public PR on the Tour Repo (portfolio)
commit: Show up to 2/3 of sessions minimum
One honest retrospective at the end

faq.title: FAQ — Vibe Code Tours
faq.description: Frequently asked questions about Vibe Code Tours: is it free, certificates, time commitment, joining from abroad, and more.
faq.heading: Frequently asked questions

items.a: Yes — free for students, full stop. Right now the Tour is bootstrapped by the instructor; we don't have a corporate sponsor yet (we're looking — see the Sponsors page). You'll never be charged. If you finish and found it valuable, the best way to give back isn't money — it's contributing to the Tour Repo: open a PR, suggest improvements, share feedback, or build and share your own project. That helps the next cohort more than any donation could.
We tour the AI coding ecosystem — multiple tools, multiple workflows, multiple model providers. Not a bootcamp on one stack.

Finish the Tour and find out. We don't lead with a certificate — a PDF isn't why you came. But complete the journey and there's a hidden gem waiting at the end for those who make it through. Along the way you also earn the things that actually carry weight: a public PR on the Tour Repo (your portfolio), a demo day recording (your work, shareable), and a reference letter on request. The gem is the reward; the work is the proof.

items.q: Can I join from outside Myanmar?
items.a: Yes. Instruction is English. Cohort schedule is Myanmar evening / SE Asia evening, which works for ~half the planet.

items.q: How much time do I need per week?
items.a: 5-8 hours outside the 4 hours of class. Honest answer: closer to 8 if you're newer.

items.q: What if I can't make a session?
items.a: Up to one-third of sessions can be missed without penalty (recordings provided). Beyond that you risk losing your cohort spot.

items.q: What if I don't have a project idea?
items.a: That's normal for beginners. Chapter 3 helps you find one. Many students enter with no idea and leave with a working prototype.

items.q: Will you teach a specific tool?
items.a: We touch on Claude Code (deeply), plus Cursor, Copilot, Aider, and the broader landscape. We don't promise depth on any single tool besides Claude Code.

items.q: What if I don't speak English well?
items.a: We provide Burmese support in channel for questions. Course materials are English; we recommend Claude itself for translation help — meta, but it works.
No. We teach AI-paired CODING. Claude is the AI; you're the coder. We don't teach how to build models — we teach how to ship with them.
If Cohort 1 goes well. Subscribe via the apply page.
See the sponsors page for organization-level support or individual donations. Or share this site with one developer who'd benefit.

setup.title: Setup Guide — Vibe Code Tours
setup.description: Get your machine ready before Chapter 1 — Linux, macOS, or Windows (WSL). Node, Python, Git, Claude Code, editor + extensions.
setup.intro1: Get your machine ready before Chapter 1. ~45–60 min (90 if new to the terminal). Works on Linux, macOS, and Windows (via WSL).
setup.intro2: You'll install: a code editor, Node, Python, Git, and Claude Code — then verify it all.
setup.fastIntro: Comfortable in a terminal? This installs everything + verifies:
setup.fastTarget: Safe to re-run. At the end you want: Passed: 6, Failed: 0.
setup.fastGist: Source and this guide live in the GitHub repo.
setup.fastWindows: Windows: install WSL first (Step 0), then run that inside Ubuntu. The script doesn't do the editor (Step 5) or GitHub SSH (Step 6) — do those manually.
setup.s0Body: Native Windows isn't supported directly — you run everything inside WSL (real Linux inside Windows). In PowerShell (Admin):
setup.s0After: Reboot, open Ubuntu from Start. Everything below runs there. macOS / Linux: skip this step.
setup.s1Note: #1 setup mistake: forgetting to close + reopen the terminal after installing nvm. Do it.
setup.s4Login: Logging in is a one-time step on first launch. Once you land in the chat, type your first message and press Enter — your “first contact”:
setup.s4Note: A friendly reply means your install, network, and account all work.
setup.s5Intro: Pick one (both free, both VS Code-based, so the same extensions work in either):
setup.s5Forks: Forks like Antigravity may use the Open VSX registry — all the above are on both, so search-and-install works the same.
setup.s6Intro: The riskiest step — don't skip the verification.
setup.s6Never: Never share the private key (~/.ssh/id_ed25519, the file without .pub).
setup.s7Intro: Run the verifier (the fast-path script does this automatically):
setup.troubleStuck: Still stuck? Post the exact error in the cohort channel #setup-help. The exact error is easy to help; "it doesn't work" is not.
setup.ossH: Optional — other AI coding tools (free / open-source)
setup.ossIntro: Not required. Claude Code is our default, but the Tour is about the landscape — Chapter 7 explores alternatives. All free + open-source; each needs its own provider account (some free tiers).
setup.ossNote: Also worth knowing (not on our install list): Aider and llm — open-source CLIs that run local open models via Ollama, fully free + offline. We point to them in Chapter 7. Don't install everything day one — pick one, form an opinion.
doneList: Claude Code, running its first command
setup.doneTax: That's the one-time tax. Pay it once, then never touch it again. See you in Chapter 1.

editors.why: Errors shown inline, not just underlined
The Tour writes a lot of markdown
Manage PRs without leaving the editor

trouble.f: Close terminal, open new one. Or source ~/.bashrc (mac: ~/.zshrc).
Close terminal, open new one. ~90% of cases.

trouble.s: command not found right after install
trouble.f: Close terminal, open new one — PATH updates on new shells.
You must run inside WSL/Ubuntu, not native Windows.
Re-check you pasted the .pub key into GitHub, right account.
Drop sudo — use brew install instead.
Antivirus or corporate proxy — try a personal network.

setup.s4PathB: If Path A fails (some networks block claude.ai), use the npm fallback:
setup.s4Cost: Cost — Claude Code is paid only: Anthropic API (Sonnet ~$3/$15 per M tokens) or Claude Pro $20/mo. No free tier.
setup.s5opIntro: opencode is an open-source, model-agnostic CLI agent (works with Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, local models). Installed alongside Claude Code so you have a working agent even when Claude is unreachable.
setup.s5opPathB: If the official installer fails, use the npm fallback:
setup.s5opAfter: First run opens an interactive picker for provider/model. We will publish a config snippet for our shared endpoint when it is live — drop it into ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json.
setup.s5opCost: Cost — opencode itself is free; you pay whatever model you point it at. Cheapest free path day 1: opencode + a Google AI Studio API key (free Gemini Flash-Lite quota ~1,500 req/day).
setup.pxH: Step 6 — Connect to the Vibe proxy
setup.pxIntro: Claude Code and opencode reach our shared proxy with your personal key. Get your key from the cohort bot (Telegram or Discord — send /mykey), then run the setup script: it downloads, applies your key, and points both tools at the proxy.
setup.pxAfter: Done — both tools now route through the Vibe proxy. Start coding:
setup.pxNote: source applies it to your current shell. To undo later and restore your personal Claude login: bash api-setup.sh --restore

repoAccess.title: Get Your Team Repo — Vibe Code Tours
repoAccess.description: How to access your team's private GitHub repo: who can join, the /repo-access command, and what's inside.
repoAccess.heading: Get your team repo
repoAccess.intro: Every team has its own private GitHub repo — team-01 to team-20. It holds your proposals, your team spec, and an automatic assignment check. Only your team and the mentors can see it.

gate.heading: Who can get in
items: You've passed Chapter 1 — that's when your GitHub account gets verified and linked.

steps.heading: How to get access
items: The bot replies with your private repo link (only you can see the reply).
Accept the GitHub invite — check your email or your GitHub notifications.

inside.heading: What's inside
items: member-proposals/ — your Vibe Code Spec (Gist, Story, Why, Why Not, Tech Spec, Definition of Done).
An automatic check on every push — green means your assignment is structurally complete.

items.a: You're not assigned to a team. Ask a mentor in your channel.
Submit Chapter 1 with /ch1 and your gist URL. Once it passes, run /repo-access again.
Your Chapter 1 submission is what links your GitHub. Submit with /ch1 <your gist URL>.
Accept it in your GitHub email or notifications. After that you can push to the repo.

repoAccess.cta: Stuck? Ask in your team channel or #help.
